items.question_text: When replying to aircraft with similar-sounding identifications, what identification should be used?
items.explanation: Paragraph 2-4-20 requires using the full identification in replies to aircraft with similar-sounding identifications. Abbreviated or same-as-pilot identification may be used for other aircraft as allowed after communications are established.

choices.text: Use the full identification.
Use only the last three characters in all replies.
Use the aircraft type only.
Use the pilot's first call exactly, even if it is abbreviated and similar-sounding traffic exists.

items.question_text: For initial ATC communication to a U.S. registry civil aircraft, which prefix is used before the registration characters?
items.explanation: For U.S. registry civil aircraft, the controller states the prefix 'November' when establishing initial communications, followed by the ICAO phonetic pronunciation of the registration numbers and letters.

choices.text: Tango.
November.
Civil.
United States.

items.question_text: How are aircraft with an ICAO three-letter designator and FAA-authorized call sign normally identified?
items.explanation: The rule states the call sign followed by the flight number in group form, while allowing single digits if clarity requires.

choices.text: Registration number only, without the company call sign.
Manufacturer name followed by the route number.
Call sign followed by the flight number in group form.
The word 'November' followed by the flight number for all air carriers.

items.question_text: For air taxi or commercial operators without FAA-authorized call signs, what prefix is used on initial contact if the pilot uses it?
items.explanation: For air taxi and commercial operators that do not have FAA-authorized call signs, the paragraph uses the prefix 'TANGO' on initial contact if used by the pilot, followed by the registration number.

choices.text: NOVEMBER.
HEAVY.
AIR TAXI only, never TANGO.
TANGO.

items.situation: Two aircraft on frequency have similar-sounding identifications. One pilot uses an abbreviated registration in the initial callup.
items.explanation: Similar-sounding identifications require full identification in reply. The controller should not rely on a shortened form that could worsen confusion.

choices.text: Use the full identification in replies to the similar-sounding aircraft.
Use only the abbreviated registration because the pilot used it first.
Use only aircraft type because similar registrations are not relevant once type is known.
Avoid replying until one aircraft leaves the frequency.

items.situation: You are establishing initial communications with a U.S.-registered civil aircraft N1234G.
items.explanation: For initial communication to a U.S. registry civil aircraft, use 'November' followed by the ICAO phonetic pronunciation of the registration numbers and letters.

choices.text: Tango One Two Three Four Golf.
November One Two Three Four Golf.
Civil One Two Three Four Golf.
One Two Three Four Golf only, because the November prefix is prohibited initially.
